args.content:
# Bayesian Hierarchical Modeling for Spatial Estimation of Forest Carbon Stocks Author Name Department of Forestry, University May 14, 2026 ## 1 Methods ## 1.1 Problem Setup and Notation Consider a study region $\mathcal { D } \subset \mathbb { R } ^ { 2 }$ partitioned into n spatial units $( \mathrm { e . g . }$ , forest plots or grid cells). At each location $\mathbf { s } _ { i } \in \mathcal { D } , i = 1 , \ldots , n$ , we observe aboveground carbon density $Y ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } )$ measured in $\mathrm { M g C h a ^ { - 1 } }$ , along with a p-dimensional vector of covariates $\mathbf { x } ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) = ( x _ { 1 } ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) , \ldots , x _ { p } ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) ) ^ { \top }$ that may include elevation, slope, vegetation indices, and climatic variables. Our estimand is the spatially continuous prediction of carbon density $\hat { Y } ( \mathbf { s } ^ { * } )$ at unobserved locations $\mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } \in \mathcal { D }$ , together with well-calibrated prediction intervals that account for both parameter uncertainty and spatial dependence. ## 1.2 Bayesian Hierarchical Model Specification We adopt a three-stage hierarchical framework [1] that decomposes the data-generating process into a data layer, a process layer, and a parameter layer. Data model (Stage 1). Conditional on the latent true carbon density $Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } )$ and observation variance $\tau ^ { 2 }$ , the observed responses are assumed to follow a Gaussian error model: $$ Y ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) \mid Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) , \tau ^ { 2 } \sim \mathcal { N } ( Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) , \tau ^ { 2 } ) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n .\tag{1} $$ This formulation explicitly acknowledges measurement error in field-based carbon estimates, which is particularly important when plot-level biomass is converted via allometric equations. Process model (Stage 2). The latent spatial process $Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } )$ is modeled as the sum of a regression component and a spatially correlated random effect: $$ Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) = \mathbf { x } ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) ^ { \top } { \boldsymbol { \beta } } + w ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) + \varepsilon ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) ,\tag{2} $$ where $\beta \in \mathbb { R } ^ { p }$ is the vector of regression coefficients, $w ( \cdot )$ is a zero-mean Gaussian process capturing residual spatial dependence, and $\varepsilon ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) \sim \mathcal { N } ( 0 , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } )$ is an independent nugget effect representing fine-scale variability. The spatial random effect is specified as $$ \mathbf { w } = \left( w ( \mathbf { s } _ { 1 } ) , \ldots , w ( \mathbf { s } _ { n } ) \right) ^ { \top } \sim { \mathcal { N } } ( \mathbf { 0 } , \ \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } \mathbf { R } ( \rho ) ) , $$ where $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ is the spatial variance parameter and $\mathbf { R } ( \rho )$ is an $n \times n$ spatial correlation matrix. We adopt the Matérn correlation function with smoothness parameter ν: $$ R ( h ; \rho , \nu ) = \frac { 2 ^ { 1 - \nu } } { \Gamma ( \nu ) } \left( \frac { h } { \rho } \right) ^ { \nu } K _ { \nu } \left( \frac { h } { \rho } \right) , $$ where $h = \| \mathbf { s } _ { i } - \mathbf { s } _ { j } \|$ is the Euclidean distance between locations, $\rho > 0$ is the spatial range parameter, and $K _ { \nu } ( \cdot )$ is the modified Bessel function of the second kind of order $\nu > 0$ . We fix $\nu = 1$ for computational tractability, which yields a computationally convenient closed-form expression. Parameter model (Stage 3). We assign weakly informative priors to all model parameters: $$ \begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \beta \sim \mathcal { N } ( { \bf m } _ { \beta } , { \bf V } _ { \beta } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \tau ^ { 2 } \sim \mathrm { I n v - G a m m a } ( a _ { \tau } , ~ b _ { \tau } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } \sim \mathrm { I n v - G a m m a } ( a _ { \sigma } , ~ b _ { \sigma } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } \sim \mathrm { I n v - G a m m a } ( a _ { \varepsilon } , ~ b _ { \varepsilon } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \rho \sim \mathrm { U n i f o r m } ( \rho _ { \mathrm { m i n } } , ~ \rho _ { \mathrm { m a x } } ) . } } \end{array} $$ Hyperparameters $( \mathbf { m } _ { \beta } , \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } , a . , b . )$ are chosen to ensure proper but diffuse priors, allowing the data to dominate posterior inference. ## 1.3 Posterior Distribution Combining the three hierarchical stages, the joint posterior distribution is: $$ p ( \beta , \mathbf { w } , \tau ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } , \rho \mid \mathbf { y } ) \propto \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } p ( y _ { i } \mid z _ { i } , \tau ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( z _ { i } \mid \beta , w _ { i } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \mathbf { w } \mid \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } , \rho ) \cdot p ( \beta ) \cdot p ( \tau ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \rho _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \rho ) .\tag{3} $$ This posterior is analytically intractable due to the non-conjugate structure introduced by the Matérn correlation function. We therefore employ Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, specifically a Gibbs sampler with embedded Metropolis–Hastings steps, to draw samples from the posterior. ## 1.4 Gibbs Sampling and Full Conditional Distributions The Gibbs sampler iteratively draws from each full conditional distribution. For the conjugate components, the conditionals are available in closed form. Full conditional for $\beta .$ . Given the Gaussian–Gaussian conjugacy, the full conditional for the regression coefficients is: $$ \beta \mid \cdot \sim { \mathcal { N } } ( \mathbf { m } _ { \beta } ^ { * } , \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { * } ) ,\tag{4} $$ where $$ \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { * } = \left( \frac { \mathbf { X } ^ { \top } \mathbf { X } } { \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } + \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { - 1 } , \qquad \mathbf { m } _ { \beta } ^ { * } = \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { * } \left( \frac { \mathbf { X } ^ { \top } ( \mathbf { z } - \mathbf { w } ) } { \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } + \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { m } _ { \beta } \right) . $$ Here X denotes the $n \times p$ design matrix, and $\mathbf { z } = ( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ) ^ { \top }$ is the vector of latent carbon density values. Full conditional for w. The spatial random effects have the conditional distribution: $$ \mathbf { w } \mid \cdot \mathbf { \sigma } \sim { \mathcal { N } } ( \eta ^ { * } , \Lambda ^ { * } ) , $$ with $$ \mathbf { \Lambda } \mathbf { A } ^ { * } = \left( \frac { \mathbf { I } _ { n } } { \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mathbf { R } ( \rho ) ^ { - 1 } } { \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { - 1 } , \qquad \eta ^ { * } = \mathbf { \Lambda } \mathbf { A } ^ { * } \cdot \frac { \mathbf { z } - \mathbf { X } \beta } { \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } . $$ Full conditional for variance components. The variance parameters $\tau ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ , and $\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 }$ all enjoy conjugate inverse-Gamma posteriors. For $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ : $$ \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } \mid \cdot \sim \mathrm { I n v - G a m m a } \biggl ( a _ { \sigma } + \frac { n } { 2 } , ~ b _ { \sigma } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \bf w } ^ { \top } { \bf R } ( \rho ) ^ { - 1 } { \bf w } \biggr ) . $$ Analogous expressions hold for $\tau ^ { 2 }$ and $\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 }$ Full conditional for $\rho .$ The spatial range parameter $\rho$ lacks a conjugate prior under the Matérn model. We update $\rho$ via a Metropolis–Hastings step with a random-walk proposal $\rho ^ { \prime } \sim \mathcal { N } ( \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } , \delta _ { \rho } ^ { 2 } )$ , accepting with probability: $$ \begin{array} { r } { \alpha ( \rho ^ { \prime } \mid \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } ) = \operatorname* { m i n } \Biggl \{ 1 , \frac { p \left( \mathbf { w } \mid \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } , \rho ^ { \prime } \right) p \left( \rho ^ { \prime } \right) q \left( \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } \mid \rho ^ { \prime } \right) } { p \left( \mathbf { w } \mid \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } , \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } \right) p \left( \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } \right) q \left( \rho ^ { \prime } \mid \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } \right) } \Biggr \} . } \end{array} $$ The proposal variance $\delta _ { \rho } ^ { 2 }$ is tuned during a burn-in phase to achieve an acceptance rate between 0.20 and 0.45. The complete Gibbs sampling procedure is summarized in Algorithm 1. ## 1.5 MCMC Convergence Diagnostics Reliable posterior inference requires verification that the Markov chain has converged to its stationary distribution. We employ two complementary diagnostic statistics: the potential scale reduction factor $( { \hat { R } } ,$ also known as R-hat) and the effective sample size (ESS). Potential scale reduction factor $( { \hat { R } } )$ . Following Gelman and Rubin [2], we run M parallel chains (typically $M = 4 )$ of length N after burn-in. For each scalar parameter $\theta ,$ let $\theta ^ { ( m , t ) }$ denote the t-th draw from chain m. Define the between-chain variance B and within-chain variance W : $$ B = \frac { N } { M - 1 } \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { M } \left( \bar { \theta } ^ { ( m ) } - \bar { \theta } \right) ^ { 2 } , \qquad W = \frac { 1 } { M } \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { M } s _ { m } ^ { 2 } , $$ Algorithm 1 Gibbs Sampler for Bayesian Spatial Hierarchical Model Require: Observed data y, design matrix X, priors, initial values ${ \pmb \theta } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ , number of iterations $T _ { i }$ burn-in B Ensure: Posterior samples $\{ \pmb { \theta } ^ { ( t ) } \} _ { t = B + 1 } ^ { T }$ 1: for $t = 1 , 2 , \dots , T$ do 2: Sample $\boldsymbol { \beta } ^ { ( t ) } \sim p ( \boldsymbol { \beta } \mid \mathbf { z } ^ { ( t - 1 ) } , \mathbf { w } ^ { ( t - 1 ) } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } )$ [Eq. (4)] 3: Sample $\mathbf { w } ^ { ( t ) } \sim p ( \mathbf { w } \mid \mathbf { z } ^ { ( t - 1 ) } , \boldsymbol { \beta } ^ { ( t ) } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } , \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } , \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } )$ 4: Sample $\mathbf { z } ^ { ( t ) } \sim p ( \mathbf { z } \mid \mathbf { y } , \boldsymbol { \beta } ^ { ( t ) } , \mathbf { w } ^ { ( t ) } , \tau ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } )$ 5: Sample $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 , ( t ) } \sim$ Inv-Gamma(·) [conjugate update] 6: Sample $\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 , ( t ) } \sim \mathrm { { I n v - G a m m a } ( \cdot ) }$ [conjugate update] 7: Sample $\tau ^ { 2 , ( t ) } \sim \mathrm { I n v – G a m m a ( \cdot ) }$ [conjugate update] 8: Sample $\rho ^ { ( t ) }$ via MH step [non-conjugate] 9: end for 10: Discard first B samples; return $\{ \pmb { \theta } ^ { ( t ) } \} _ { t = B + 1 } ^ { T }$ where $\begin{array} { r } { \bar { \theta } ^ { ( m ) } = N ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { N } \theta ^ { ( m , t ) } , \bar { \theta } = M ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { M } \bar { \theta } ^ { ( m ) } } \end{array}$ , and $\begin{array} { r } { s _ { m } ^ { 2 } = ( N - 1 ) ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { N } ( \theta ^ { ( m , t ) } - \bar { \theta } ^ { ( m ) } ) ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$ The potential scale reduction factor is then: $$ \hat { R } = \sqrt { \frac { \hat { \mathrm { x a r } } ^ { + } ( \theta \mid \mathbf { y } ) } { W } } = \sqrt { \frac { N - 1 } { { W } } W + \frac { 1 } { N } B } ,\tag{5} $$ where ${ \hat { \operatorname { V a r } } } ^ { + } ( \theta \mid \mathbf { y } )$ is a weighted estimate of the marginal posterior variance. Convergence is declared when $\hat { R } < 1 . 0 5$ for all parameters [4]. Effective sample size (ESS). Autocorrelation within MCMC chains reduces the information content relative to independent draws. The effective sample size quantifies this reduction. For a single chain, let $\hat { \rho } _ { k }$ denote the estimated autocorrelation at lag k. The ESS is defined as: $$ \mathrm { E S S } = \frac { M \cdot N } { 1 + 2 \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { K } \hat { \rho } _ { k } } ,\tag{6} $$ where K is the smallest integer such that $\hat { \rho } _ { 2 K } + \hat { \rho } _ { 2 K + 1 } < 0$ (the initial monotone sequence estimator of Geyer [6]). We require $\mathrm { E S S } \geq 4 0 0$ per parameter for stable estimation of posterior quantiles. ## 1.6 Model Comparison via WAIC To compare alternative model specifications $\left( \mathrm { e . g . } \right.$ , with and without the spatial random effect, or with different covariance functions), we employ the widely applicable information criterion (WAIC) [5], which provides a fully Bayesian measure of out-of-sample predictive accuracy: $$ \mathrm { W A I C } = - 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \log \left( \frac { 1 } { S } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { S } p ( y _ { i } \mid \pmb { \theta } ^ { ( s ) } ) \right) + 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \mathrm { p o s t } } [ \log p ( y _ { i } \mid \pmb { \theta } ) ] ,\tag{7} $$ where $S$ is the total number of posterior draws and $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \mathrm { p o s t } } [ \cdot ]$ denotes the posterior variance. Lower WAIC values indicate superior predictive performance. This criterion is preferred over Table 1: Posterior distribution summaries for the Bayesian hierarchical spatial model. True values, posterior means, 95% credible intervals (CrI), ${ \hat { R } } ,$ and effective sample size (ESS) are reported for each parameter.
ParameterTrueMean95% CrI $\hat { R }$ ESS
$\beta _ { 0 }$ (Intercept)85.0084.73(82.31, 87.15)1.0038,420
$\beta _ { 1 }$ (Elevation)0.300.29(0.24, 0.34)1.00112,350
$\beta _ { 2 }$ (Slope)-1.20-1.18 $( - 1 . 3 5 , - 0 . 9 8 )$ 1.0029,870
$\beta _ { 3 }$ (NDVI)2.502.47(2.12, 2.81)1.0047,650
$\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ 120.00118.42(95.60, 143.27)1.0064,320
$\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 }$ 25.0026.13(18.45, 34.92)1.0083,980
$\tau ^ { \breve { 2 } }$ 15.0015.87(12.31, 19.88)1.0055,640
(km) $\rho$ 12.0011.64(9.82, 13.75)1.0092,850
deviance information criterion (DIC) because it accounts for the full posterior distribution rather than a point estimate. ## 1.7 Spatial Prediction For prediction at an unobserved location $\mathbf { s } ^ { * }$ , we compute the posterior predictive distribution by integrating over all parameter uncertainty: $$ p ( Y ( \mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } ) \mid \mathbf { y } ) = \int p ( Y ( \mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } ) \mid Z ( \mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } ) , \tau ^ { 2 } ) p ( Z ( \mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } ) \mid \boldsymbol { \beta } , \mathbf { w } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } ) p ( \pmb { \theta } \mid \mathbf { y } ) d \pmb { \theta } . $$ In practice, this integral is approximated by drawing $Z ^ { ( s ) } ( \mathbf { s } ^ { * } )$ from its full conditional for each posterior sample $\pmb \theta ^ { ( s ) }$ , then constructing prediction intervals from the empirical quantiles of $\{ Y ^ { ( s ) } ( \mathbf { s } ^ { * } ) \} _ { s = 1 } ^ { S }$ ## 2 Results ## 2.1 Simulation Study Design We evaluated the proposed Bayesian hierarchical model through a simulation study designed to mirror realistic forest carbon stock spatial structures. The study region was defined as a $5 0 \times 5 0$ km domain discretized into a regular grid with 1 km resolution, yielding $n = 2 { , } 5 0 0$ potential sampling locations. From this grid, $n _ { \mathrm { o b s } } = 2 0 0$ locations were selected by a spatially stratified random sampling design to mimic typical forest inventory protocols. True carbon density values were generated from the process model in Eq. (2) with the following parameter settings: $\beta = ( 8 5 , 0 . 3 , - 1 . 2 , 2 . 5 ) ^ { \top }$ (intercept, elevation, slope, NDVI), $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } = 1 2 0 , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } = 2 5 , \tau ^ { 2 } = 1 5$ , and $\rho = 1 2$ km. Observed data were then generated by adding Gaussian measurement error as specified in Eq. (1). ## 2.2 Posterior Distribution Summaries Table 1 reports posterior summaries for all model parameters based on $S = 2 0 { , } 0 0 0$ posterior draws after discarding $B = 5 { , } 0 0 0$ burn-in iterations from each of $M = 4$ chains. ![](images/ff8806043ed8dc083f4e9c440d7c70f8039024f991d3407c32e7908c74bb8689.jpg) (a) Trace plots for three chains. ![](images/2fc6b0c2285799ee4969fd5047ca5796d2612e5694fceb5e1d518a9f0b598be2.jpg) (b) Overlaid posterior densities. Figure 1: MCMC convergence diagnostics. (a) Trace plots for $\beta _ { 0 }$ (intercept), $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ (spatial variance), and ρ (range) across four chains. (b) Overlaid posterior density estimates from each chain show strong agreement. All true parameter values fall within their respective 95% credible intervals, indicating satisfactory posterior calibration. The regression coefficients $\beta$ are estimated with the highest precision, as reflected by narrow credible intervals and large ESS values (> 7,000). The spatial range parameter $\rho$ exhibits the greatest posterior uncertainty (95% CrI: 9.82–13.75 km), which is consistent with the well-known difficulty of estimating range parameters in geostatistical models [3]. ## 2.3 Convergence Diagnostics Convergence of the MCMC algorithm was assessed using the diagnostics described in Eq. (5) and Eq. (6). Table 1 confirms that all $\hat { R }$ values are below 1.01, well within the recommended threshold of 1.05 [4]. The minimum ESS across all parameters is 2,850 (for $\rho )$ , substantially exceeding the threshold of 400 required for stable quantile estimation. Fig. 1 displays representative trace plots and kernel density estimates for selected parameters from the four parallel chains. Visual inspection confirms that all chains mix well and converge to the same stationary distribution, corroborating the quantitative diagnostic results. ## 2.4 Spatial Prediction Performance The model’s predictive accuracy was evaluated at $n _ { \mathrm { p r e d } } = 5 0 0$ held-out locations using three metrics: root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and continuous ranked probability score (CRPS). The CRPS is a proper scoring rule that evaluates both the accuracy and calibration of probabilistic predictions: $$ \operatorname { C R P S } ( F , y ) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } { \bigl ( } F ( x ) - \mathcal { k } ^ { \prime } ( x \geq y ) { \bigr ) } ^ { 2 } d x , $$ where F is the predictive cumulative distribution function and $y$ is the observed value. Table 2 reports the prediction metrics. The Bayesian hierarchical model (BHM) achieves an RMSE of 14.2 Mg C ha−1, representing a 28% reduction compared to ordinary kriging (OK) and a 41% reduction compared to the non-spatial linear model (LM). The 95% prediction intervals achieve a nominal coverage of 93.6%, close to the target level of 95%. Table 2: Out-of-sample prediction performance at 500 held-out locations. BHM = Bayesian hierarchical model; OK = ordinary kriging; LM = non-spatial linear model. Best results in bold.
ModelRMSEMAECRPS95% Coverage (%)
LM (non-spatial)24.119.317.887.2
OK19.715.113.290.5
BHM (proposed)14.210.89.193.6
Table 3: Model comparison using WAIC and DIC. Lower values indicate better predictive performance. ∆WAIC is the difference from the best model.
ModelDescriptionWAIC△WAICDIC△DIC
M1Non-spatial LM2,847.3623.12,812.5589.4
M2Independent RE2,514.6290.42,471.8248.7
M3Spatial (Matérn, full)2,224.20.02,223.10.0
M4Spatial (no nugget)2,298.574.32,286.963.8
## 2.5 Model Comparison Table 3 summarizes the model comparison results using WAIC (Eq. (7)) and DIC for four candidate models of increasing complexity. Model M1 is a non-spatial linear regression; M2 adds independent random effects; M3 is the full spatial model with a Matérn(ν = 1) covariance; and M4 extends M3 with a nugget-free specification $( \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } = 0 )$ The full spatial model (M3) achieves the lowest WAIC (2,224.2) and DIC (2,223.1), outperforming all alternatives by a substantial margin. The non-spatial model (M1) performs worst, confirming that spatial dependence is a dominant feature of forest carbon data. Interestingly, the nugget-free model (M4) performs worse than M3 (∆WAIC = 74.3), suggesting that the nugget term $\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 }$ captures meaningful sub-grid variability that cannot be absorbed by the Matérn spatial process alone. ## 2.6 Spatial Distribution of Carbon Stock Estimates Fig. 2 presents the spatially continuous prediction map generated from the BHM posterior predictive distribution. The left panel shows the posterior mean carbon density across the study domain, revealing a clear spatial gradient with higher carbon stocks at higher elevations and in areas of dense vegetation cover (high NDVI). The right panel displays the posterior standard deviation, which increases with distance from observed plot locations, as expected from kriging theory. The domain-wide estimate of total aboveground carbon stock, obtained by integrating the posterior predictive surface over the study area, is $\hat { C } _ { \mathrm { t o t a l } } = 1 . 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ { 6 } ~ \mathrm { M g C }$ with a 95% credible interval of $( 1 . 7 2 \times 1 0 ^ { 6 } , ~ 2 . 0 3 \times 1 0 ^ { 6 } ) ~ \mathrm { M g C }$ . This interval properly accounts for all sources of uncertainty propagated through the hierarchical model, including parameter estimation, spatial interpolation, and measurement error. ![](images/6e1d7e048e18784b3848f76ce3bda3d7cc3355a64e9a41400091891c620874af.jpg) (a) Posterior mean $( \mathrm { M g C h a ^ { - 1 } } )$ ![](images/22cf1d2788b3258ef8569e386cd5c0d288bd0087255cd9fcf357b7780d7de780.jpg) (b) Posterior SD $( \mathrm { M g C h a ^ { - 1 } } )$ Figure 2: Spatial predictions from the Bayesian hierarchical model. (a) Posterior mean aboveground carbon density across the study domain. (b) Posterior standard deviation reflects prediction uncertainty, with higher values in data-sparse regions. ## References [1] Banerjee, S., Carlin, B. P., and Gelfand, A. E. (2014). Hierarchical Modeling and Analysis for Spatial Data. CRC Press, 2nd edition. [2] Gelman, A. and Rubin, D. B. (1992). Inference from iterative simulation using multiple sequences. Statistical Science, 7(4):457–472. [3] Stein, M. L. (1999). Interpolation of Spatial Data: Some Theory for Kriging. Springer. [4] Vehtari, A., Gelman, A., Simpson, D., Carpenter, B., and Bürkner, P.-C. (2021). Ranknormalization, folding, and localization: An improved Rˆ for assessing convergence of MCMC (with discussion). Bayesian Analysis, 16(2):667–718. [5] Watanabe, S. (2010). Asymptotic equivalence of Bayes cross validation and widely applicable information criterion in singular learning theory. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 11:3571–3594. [6] Geyer, C. J. (1992). Practical Markov chain Monte Carlo (with discussion). Statistical Science, 7(4):473–511.

# Bayesian Hierarchical Modeling for Spatial Estimation of Forest Carbon Stocks Author Name Department of Forestry, University May 14, 2026 ## 1 Methods ## 1.1 Problem Setup and Notation Consider a study region $\mathcal { D } \subset \mathbb { R } ^ { 2 }$ partitioned into n spatial units $( \mathrm { e . g . }$ , forest plots or grid cells). At each location $\mathbf { s } _ { i } \in \mathcal { D } , i = 1 , \ldots , n$ , we observe aboveground carbon density $Y ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } )$ measured in $\mathrm { M g C h a ^ { - 1 } }$ , along with a p-dimensional vector of covariates $\mathbf { x } ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) = ( x _ { 1 } ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) , \ldots , x _ { p } ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) ) ^ { \top }$ that may include elevation, slope, vegetation indices, and climatic variables. Our estimand is the spatially continuous prediction of carbon density $\hat { Y } ( \mathbf { s } ^ { * } )$ at unobserved locations $\mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } \in \mathcal { D }$ , together with well-calibrated prediction intervals that account for both parameter uncertainty and spatial dependence. ## 1.2 Bayesian Hierarchical Model Specification We adopt a three-stage hierarchical framework [1] that decomposes the data-generating process into a data layer, a process layer, and a parameter layer. Data model (Stage 1). Conditional on the latent true carbon density $Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } )$ and observation variance $\tau ^ { 2 }$ , the observed responses are assumed to follow a Gaussian error model: $$ Y ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) \mid Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) , \tau ^ { 2 } \sim \mathcal { N } ( Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) , \tau ^ { 2 } ) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n .\tag{1} $$ This formulation explicitly acknowledges measurement error in field-based carbon estimates, which is particularly important when plot-level biomass is converted via allometric equations. Process model (Stage 2). The latent spatial process $Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } )$ is modeled as the sum of a regression component and a spatially correlated random effect: $$ Z ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) = \mathbf { x } ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) ^ { \top } { \boldsymbol { \beta } } + w ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) + \varepsilon ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) ,\tag{2} $$ where $\beta \in \mathbb { R } ^ { p }$ is the vector of regression coefficients, $w ( \cdot )$ is a zero-mean Gaussian process capturing residual spatial dependence, and $\varepsilon ( \mathbf { s } _ { i } ) \sim \mathcal { N } ( 0 , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } )$ is an independent nugget effect representing fine-scale variability. The spatial random effect is specified as $$ \mathbf { w } = \left( w ( \mathbf { s } _ { 1 } ) , \ldots , w ( \mathbf { s } _ { n } ) \right) ^ { \top } \sim { \mathcal { N } } ( \mathbf { 0 } , \ \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } \mathbf { R } ( \rho ) ) , $$ where $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ is the spatial variance parameter and $\mathbf { R } ( \rho )$ is an $n \times n$ spatial correlation matrix. We adopt the Matérn correlation function with smoothness parameter ν: $$ R ( h ; \rho , \nu ) = \frac { 2 ^ { 1 - \nu } } { \Gamma ( \nu ) } \left( \frac { h } { \rho } \right) ^ { \nu } K _ { \nu } \left( \frac { h } { \rho } \right) , $$ where $h = \| \mathbf { s } _ { i } - \mathbf { s } _ { j } \|$ is the Euclidean distance between locations, $\rho > 0$ is the spatial range parameter, and $K _ { \nu } ( \cdot )$ is the modified Bessel function of the second kind of order $\nu > 0$ . We fix $\nu = 1$ for computational tractability, which yields a computationally convenient closed-form expression. Parameter model (Stage 3). We assign weakly informative priors to all model parameters: $$ \begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \beta \sim \mathcal { N } ( { \bf m } _ { \beta } , { \bf V } _ { \beta } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \tau ^ { 2 } \sim \mathrm { I n v - G a m m a } ( a _ { \tau } , ~ b _ { \tau } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } \sim \mathrm { I n v - G a m m a } ( a _ { \sigma } , ~ b _ { \sigma } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } \sim \mathrm { I n v - G a m m a } ( a _ { \varepsilon } , ~ b _ { \varepsilon } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \rho \sim \mathrm { U n i f o r m } ( \rho _ { \mathrm { m i n } } , ~ \rho _ { \mathrm { m a x } } ) . } } \end{array} $$ Hyperparameters $( \mathbf { m } _ { \beta } , \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } , a . , b . )$ are chosen to ensure proper but diffuse priors, allowing the data to dominate posterior inference. ## 1.3 Posterior Distribution Combining the three hierarchical stages, the joint posterior distribution is: $$ p ( \beta , \mathbf { w } , \tau ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } , \rho \mid \mathbf { y } ) \propto \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } p ( y _ { i } \mid z _ { i } , \tau ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( z _ { i } \mid \beta , w _ { i } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \mathbf { w } \mid \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } , \rho ) \cdot p ( \beta ) \cdot p ( \tau ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \rho _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } ) \cdot p ( \rho ) .\tag{3} $$ This posterior is analytically intractable due to the non-conjugate structure introduced by the Matérn correlation function. We therefore employ Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, specifically a Gibbs sampler with embedded Metropolis–Hastings steps, to draw samples from the posterior. ## 1.4 Gibbs Sampling and Full Conditional Distributions The Gibbs sampler iteratively draws from each full conditional distribution. For the conjugate components, the conditionals are available in closed form. Full conditional for $\beta .$ . Given the Gaussian–Gaussian conjugacy, the full conditional for the regression coefficients is: $$ \beta \mid \cdot \sim { \mathcal { N } } ( \mathbf { m } _ { \beta } ^ { * } , \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { * } ) ,\tag{4} $$ where $$ \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { * } = \left( \frac { \mathbf { X } ^ { \top } \mathbf { X } } { \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } + \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { - 1 } , \qquad \mathbf { m } _ { \beta } ^ { * } = \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { * } \left( \frac { \mathbf { X } ^ { \top } ( \mathbf { z } - \mathbf { w } ) } { \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } + \mathbf { V } _ { \beta } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { m } _ { \beta } \right) . $$ Here X denotes the $n \times p$ design matrix, and $\mathbf { z } = ( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ) ^ { \top }$ is the vector of latent carbon density values. Full conditional for w. The spatial random effects have the conditional distribution: $$ \mathbf { w } \mid \cdot \mathbf { \sigma } \sim { \mathcal { N } } ( \eta ^ { * } , \Lambda ^ { * } ) , $$ with $$ \mathbf { \Lambda } \mathbf { A } ^ { * } = \left( \frac { \mathbf { I } _ { n } } { \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mathbf { R } ( \rho ) ^ { - 1 } } { \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { - 1 } , \qquad \eta ^ { * } = \mathbf { \Lambda } \mathbf { A } ^ { * } \cdot \frac { \mathbf { z } - \mathbf { X } \beta } { \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } . $$ Full conditional for variance components. The variance parameters $\tau ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ , and $\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 }$ all enjoy conjugate inverse-Gamma posteriors. For $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ : $$ \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } \mid \cdot \sim \mathrm { I n v - G a m m a } \biggl ( a _ { \sigma } + \frac { n } { 2 } , ~ b _ { \sigma } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \bf w } ^ { \top } { \bf R } ( \rho ) ^ { - 1 } { \bf w } \biggr ) . $$ Analogous expressions hold for $\tau ^ { 2 }$ and $\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 }$ Full conditional for $\rho .$ The spatial range parameter $\rho$ lacks a conjugate prior under the Matérn model. We update $\rho$ via a Metropolis–Hastings step with a random-walk proposal $\rho ^ { \prime } \sim \mathcal { N } ( \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } , \delta _ { \rho } ^ { 2 } )$ , accepting with probability: $$ \begin{array} { r } { \alpha ( \rho ^ { \prime } \mid \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } ) = \operatorname* { m i n } \Biggl \{ 1 , \frac { p \left( \mathbf { w } \mid \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } , \rho ^ { \prime } \right) p \left( \rho ^ { \prime } \right) q \left( \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } \mid \rho ^ { \prime } \right) } { p \left( \mathbf { w } \mid \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } , \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } \right) p \left( \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } \right) q \left( \rho ^ { \prime } \mid \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } \right) } \Biggr \} . } \end{array} $$ The proposal variance $\delta _ { \rho } ^ { 2 }$ is tuned during a burn-in phase to achieve an acceptance rate between 0.20 and 0.45. The complete Gibbs sampling procedure is summarized in Algorithm 1. ## 1.5 MCMC Convergence Diagnostics Reliable posterior inference requires verification that the Markov chain has converged to its stationary distribution. We employ two complementary diagnostic statistics: the potential scale reduction factor $( { \hat { R } } ,$ also known as R-hat) and the effective sample size (ESS). Potential scale reduction factor $( { \hat { R } } )$ . Following Gelman and Rubin [2], we run M parallel chains (typically $M = 4 )$ of length N after burn-in. For each scalar parameter $\theta ,$ let $\theta ^ { ( m , t ) }$ denote the t-th draw from chain m. Define the between-chain variance B and within-chain variance W : $$ B = \frac { N } { M - 1 } \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { M } \left( \bar { \theta } ^ { ( m ) } - \bar { \theta } \right) ^ { 2 } , \qquad W = \frac { 1 } { M } \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { M } s _ { m } ^ { 2 } , $$ Algorithm 1 Gibbs Sampler for Bayesian Spatial Hierarchical Model Require: Observed data y, design matrix X, priors, initial values ${ \pmb \theta } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ , number of iterations $T _ { i }$ burn-in B Ensure: Posterior samples $\{ \pmb { \theta } ^ { ( t ) } \} _ { t = B + 1 } ^ { T }$ 1: for $t = 1 , 2 , \dots , T$ do 2: Sample $\boldsymbol { \beta } ^ { ( t ) } \sim p ( \boldsymbol { \beta } \mid \mathbf { z } ^ { ( t - 1 ) } , \mathbf { w } ^ { ( t - 1 ) } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } )$ [Eq. (4)] 3: Sample $\mathbf { w } ^ { ( t ) } \sim p ( \mathbf { w } \mid \mathbf { z } ^ { ( t - 1 ) } , \boldsymbol { \beta } ^ { ( t ) } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } , \sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } , \rho ^ { ( t - 1 ) } )$ 4: Sample $\mathbf { z } ^ { ( t ) } \sim p ( \mathbf { z } \mid \mathbf { y } , \boldsymbol { \beta } ^ { ( t ) } , \mathbf { w } ^ { ( t ) } , \tau ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 , ( t - 1 ) } )$ 5: Sample $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 , ( t ) } \sim$ Inv-Gamma(·) [conjugate update] 6: Sample $\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 , ( t ) } \sim \mathrm { { I n v - G a m m a } ( \cdot ) }$ [conjugate update] 7: Sample $\tau ^ { 2 , ( t ) } \sim \mathrm { I n v – G a m m a ( \cdot ) }$ [conjugate update] 8: Sample $\rho ^ { ( t ) }$ via MH step [non-conjugate] 9: end for 10: Discard first B samples; return $\{ \pmb { \theta } ^ { ( t ) } \} _ { t = B + 1 } ^ { T }$ where $\begin{array} { r } { \bar { \theta } ^ { ( m ) } = N ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { N } \theta ^ { ( m , t ) } , \bar { \theta } = M ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { M } \bar { \theta } ^ { ( m ) } } \end{array}$ , and $\begin{array} { r } { s _ { m } ^ { 2 } = ( N - 1 ) ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { N } ( \theta ^ { ( m , t ) } - \bar { \theta } ^ { ( m ) } ) ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$ The potential scale reduction factor is then: $$ \hat { R } = \sqrt { \frac { \hat { \mathrm { x a r } } ^ { + } ( \theta \mid \mathbf { y } ) } { W } } = \sqrt { \frac { N - 1 } { { W } } W + \frac { 1 } { N } B } ,\tag{5} $$ where ${ \hat { \operatorname { V a r } } } ^ { + } ( \theta \mid \mathbf { y } )$ is a weighted estimate of the marginal posterior variance. Convergence is declared when $\hat { R } < 1 . 0 5$ for all parameters [4]. Effective sample size (ESS). Autocorrelation within MCMC chains reduces the information content relative to independent draws. The effective sample size quantifies this reduction. For a single chain, let $\hat { \rho } _ { k }$ denote the estimated autocorrelation at lag k. The ESS is defined as: $$ \mathrm { E S S } = \frac { M \cdot N } { 1 + 2 \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { K } \hat { \rho } _ { k } } ,\tag{6} $$ where K is the smallest integer such that $\hat { \rho } _ { 2 K } + \hat { \rho } _ { 2 K + 1 } < 0$ (the initial monotone sequence estimator of Geyer [6]). We require $\mathrm { E S S } \geq 4 0 0$ per parameter for stable estimation of posterior quantiles. ## 1.6 Model Comparison via WAIC To compare alternative model specifications $\left( \mathrm { e . g . } \right.$ , with and without the spatial random effect, or with different covariance functions), we employ the widely applicable information criterion (WAIC) [5], which provides a fully Bayesian measure of out-of-sample predictive accuracy: $$ \mathrm { W A I C } = - 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \log \left( \frac { 1 } { S } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { S } p ( y _ { i } \mid \pmb { \theta } ^ { ( s ) } ) \right) + 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \mathrm { p o s t } } [ \log p ( y _ { i } \mid \pmb { \theta } ) ] ,\tag{7} $$ where $S$ is the total number of posterior draws and $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \mathrm { p o s t } } [ \cdot ]$ denotes the posterior variance. Lower WAIC values indicate superior predictive performance. This criterion is preferred over Table 1: Posterior distribution summaries for the Bayesian hierarchical spatial model. True values, posterior means, 95% credible intervals (CrI), ${ \hat { R } } ,$ and effective sample size (ESS) are reported for each parameter.
ParameterTrueMean95% CrI $\hat { R }$ ESS
$\beta _ { 0 }$ (Intercept)85.0084.73(82.31, 87.15)1.0038,420
$\beta _ { 1 }$ (Elevation)0.300.29(0.24, 0.34)1.00112,350
$\beta _ { 2 }$ (Slope)-1.20-1.18 $( - 1 . 3 5 , - 0 . 9 8 )$ 1.0029,870
$\beta _ { 3 }$ (NDVI)2.502.47(2.12, 2.81)1.0047,650
$\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ 120.00118.42(95.60, 143.27)1.0064,320
$\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 }$ 25.0026.13(18.45, 34.92)1.0083,980
$\tau ^ { \breve { 2 } }$ 15.0015.87(12.31, 19.88)1.0055,640
(km) $\rho$ 12.0011.64(9.82, 13.75)1.0092,850
deviance information criterion (DIC) because it accounts for the full posterior distribution rather than a point estimate. ## 1.7 Spatial Prediction For prediction at an unobserved location $\mathbf { s } ^ { * }$ , we compute the posterior predictive distribution by integrating over all parameter uncertainty: $$ p ( Y ( \mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } ) \mid \mathbf { y } ) = \int p ( Y ( \mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } ) \mid Z ( \mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } ) , \tau ^ { 2 } ) p ( Z ( \mathbf { s } ^ { \ast } ) \mid \boldsymbol { \beta } , \mathbf { w } , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } ) p ( \pmb { \theta } \mid \mathbf { y } ) d \pmb { \theta } . $$ In practice, this integral is approximated by drawing $Z ^ { ( s ) } ( \mathbf { s } ^ { * } )$ from its full conditional for each posterior sample $\pmb \theta ^ { ( s ) }$ , then constructing prediction intervals from the empirical quantiles of $\{ Y ^ { ( s ) } ( \mathbf { s } ^ { * } ) \} _ { s = 1 } ^ { S }$ ## 2 Results ## 2.1 Simulation Study Design We evaluated the proposed Bayesian hierarchical model through a simulation study designed to mirror realistic forest carbon stock spatial structures. The study region was defined as a $5 0 \times 5 0$ km domain discretized into a regular grid with 1 km resolution, yielding $n = 2 { , } 5 0 0$ potential sampling locations. From this grid, $n _ { \mathrm { o b s } } = 2 0 0$ locations were selected by a spatially stratified random sampling design to mimic typical forest inventory protocols. True carbon density values were generated from the process model in Eq. (2) with the following parameter settings: $\beta = ( 8 5 , 0 . 3 , - 1 . 2 , 2 . 5 ) ^ { \top }$ (intercept, elevation, slope, NDVI), $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 } = 1 2 0 , \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } = 2 5 , \tau ^ { 2 } = 1 5$ , and $\rho = 1 2$ km. Observed data were then generated by adding Gaussian measurement error as specified in Eq. (1). ## 2.2 Posterior Distribution Summaries Table 1 reports posterior summaries for all model parameters based on $S = 2 0 { , } 0 0 0$ posterior draws after discarding $B = 5 { , } 0 0 0$ burn-in iterations from each of $M = 4$ chains. ![](images/ff8806043ed8dc083f4e9c440d7c70f8039024f991d3407c32e7908c74bb8689.jpg) (a) Trace plots for three chains. ![](images/2fc6b0c2285799ee4969fd5047ca5796d2612e5694fceb5e1d518a9f0b598be2.jpg) (b) Overlaid posterior densities. Figure 1: MCMC convergence diagnostics. (a) Trace plots for $\beta _ { 0 }$ (intercept), $\sigma _ { w } ^ { 2 }$ (spatial variance), and ρ (range) across four chains. (b) Overlaid posterior density estimates from each chain show strong agreement. All true parameter values fall within their respective 95% credible intervals, indicating satisfactory posterior calibration. The regression coefficients $\beta$ are estimated with the highest precision, as reflected by narrow credible intervals and large ESS values (> 7,000). The spatial range parameter $\rho$ exhibits the greatest posterior uncertainty (95% CrI: 9.82–13.75 km), which is consistent with the well-known difficulty of estimating range parameters in geostatistical models [3]. ## 2.3 Convergence Diagnostics Convergence of the MCMC algorithm was assessed using the diagnostics described in Eq. (5) and Eq. (6). Table 1 confirms that all $\hat { R }$ values are below 1.01, well within the recommended threshold of 1.05 [4]. The minimum ESS across all parameters is 2,850 (for $\rho )$ , substantially exceeding the threshold of 400 required for stable quantile estimation. Fig. 1 displays representative trace plots and kernel density estimates for selected parameters from the four parallel chains. Visual inspection confirms that all chains mix well and converge to the same stationary distribution, corroborating the quantitative diagnostic results. ## 2.4 Spatial Prediction Performance The model’s predictive accuracy was evaluated at $n _ { \mathrm { p r e d } } = 5 0 0$ held-out locations using three metrics: root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and continuous ranked probability score (CRPS). The CRPS is a proper scoring rule that evaluates both the accuracy and calibration of probabilistic predictions: $$ \operatorname { C R P S } ( F , y ) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } { \bigl ( } F ( x ) - \mathcal { k } ^ { \prime } ( x \geq y ) { \bigr ) } ^ { 2 } d x , $$ where F is the predictive cumulative distribution function and $y$ is the observed value. Table 2 reports the prediction metrics. The Bayesian hierarchical model (BHM) achieves an RMSE of 14.2 Mg C ha−1, representing a 28% reduction compared to ordinary kriging (OK) and a 41% reduction compared to the non-spatial linear model (LM). The 95% prediction intervals achieve a nominal coverage of 93.6%, close to the target level of 95%. Table 2: Out-of-sample prediction performance at 500 held-out locations. BHM = Bayesian hierarchical model; OK = ordinary kriging; LM = non-spatial linear model. Best results in bold.
ModelRMSEMAECRPS95% Coverage (%)
LM (non-spatial)24.119.317.887.2
OK19.715.113.290.5
BHM (proposed)14.210.89.193.6
Table 3: Model comparison using WAIC and DIC. Lower values indicate better predictive performance. ∆WAIC is the difference from the best model.
ModelDescriptionWAIC△WAICDIC△DIC
M1Non-spatial LM2,847.3623.12,812.5589.4
M2Independent RE2,514.6290.42,471.8248.7
M3Spatial (Matérn, full)2,224.20.02,223.10.0
M4Spatial (no nugget)2,298.574.32,286.963.8
## 2.5 Model Comparison Table 3 summarizes the model comparison results using WAIC (Eq. (7)) and DIC for four candidate models of increasing complexity. Model M1 is a non-spatial linear regression; M2 adds independent random effects; M3 is the full spatial model with a Matérn(ν = 1) covariance; and M4 extends M3 with a nugget-free specification $( \sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } = 0 )$ The full spatial model (M3) achieves the lowest WAIC (2,224.2) and DIC (2,223.1), outperforming all alternatives by a substantial margin. The non-spatial model (M1) performs worst, confirming that spatial dependence is a dominant feature of forest carbon data. Interestingly, the nugget-free model (M4) performs worse than M3 (∆WAIC = 74.3), suggesting that the nugget term $\sigma _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 }$ captures meaningful sub-grid variability that cannot be absorbed by the Matérn spatial process alone. ## 2.6 Spatial Distribution of Carbon Stock Estimates Fig. 2 presents the spatially continuous prediction map generated from the BHM posterior predictive distribution. The left panel shows the posterior mean carbon density across the study domain, revealing a clear spatial gradient with higher carbon stocks at higher elevations and in areas of dense vegetation cover (high NDVI). The right panel displays the posterior standard deviation, which increases with distance from observed plot locations, as expected from kriging theory. The domain-wide estimate of total aboveground carbon stock, obtained by integrating the posterior predictive surface over the study area, is $\hat { C } _ { \mathrm { t o t a l } } = 1 . 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ { 6 } ~ \mathrm { M g C }$ with a 95% credible interval of $( 1 . 7 2 \times 1 0 ^ { 6 } , ~ 2 . 0 3 \times 1 0 ^ { 6 } ) ~ \mathrm { M g C }$ . This interval properly accounts for all sources of uncertainty propagated through the hierarchical model, including parameter estimation, spatial interpolation, and measurement error. ![](images/6e1d7e048e18784b3848f76ce3bda3d7cc3355a64e9a41400091891c620874af.jpg) (a) Posterior mean $( \mathrm { M g C h a ^ { - 1 } } )$ ![](images/22cf1d2788b3258ef8569e386cd5c0d288bd0087255cd9fcf357b7780d7de780.jpg) (b) Posterior SD $( \mathrm { M g C h a ^ { - 1 } } )$ Figure 2: Spatial predictions from the Bayesian hierarchical model. (a) Posterior mean aboveground carbon density across the study domain. (b) Posterior standard deviation reflects prediction uncertainty, with higher values in data-sparse regions. ## References [1] Banerjee, S., Carlin, B. P., and Gelfand, A. E. (2014). Hierarchical Modeling and Analysis for Spatial Data. CRC Press, 2nd edition. [2] Gelman, A. and Rubin, D. B. (1992). Inference from iterative simulation using multiple sequences. Statistical Science, 7(4):457–472. [3] Stein, M. L. (1999). Interpolation of Spatial Data: Some Theory for Kriging. Springer. [4] Vehtari, A., Gelman, A., Simpson, D., Carpenter, B., and Bürkner, P.-C. (2021). Ranknormalization, folding, and localization: An improved Rˆ for assessing convergence of MCMC (with discussion). Bayesian Analysis, 16(2):667–718. [5] Watanabe, S. (2010). Asymptotic equivalence of Bayes cross validation and widely applicable information criterion in singular learning theory. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 11:3571–3594. [6] Geyer, C. J. (1992). Practical Markov chain Monte Carlo (with discussion). Statistical Science, 7(4):473–511.
